skill.name: entra-agent-id
skill.description: Guide Microsoft Entra Agent ID setup for Agent Identity Blueprints, BlueprintPrincipals, per-instance Agent Identities, OAuth token exchange, OBO flows, and the AgentID sidecar.
skill.summary: Configure Microsoft Entra Agent ID identities and token flows for AI agents.
skill.author: microsoft

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, network, credential, and filesystem patterns. Review found these are documentation examples for Microsoft Graph and Entra Agent ID workflows, not hidden executable code or prompt injection. The skill remains medium risk because the examples create credentials, exchange tokens, and grant privileged Graph permissions when copied into an authorized environment.

medium_findings.title: Privileged Microsoft Graph Provisioning Examples
medium_findings.description: The skill provides examples that create Agent Identity Blueprints, Agent Identities, app role assignments, delegated permission grants, and admin consent. This is legitimate for the skill purpose, but unsafe use could grant broad tenant permissions to agent identities.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples clearly target real Microsoft Graph provisioning and permission APIs. The intent is administrative documentation, so the risk is legitimate-use risk rather than confirmed abuse.

medium_findings.title: Client Secret and Token Exchange Handling
medium_findings.description: The documentation shows reading Azure client secrets from environment variables, creating Blueprint password credentials, and sending client secrets to Entra token endpoints. Secrets are marked as local development, but copied examples can expose or overuse long-lived credentials.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Credential creation and token exchange are explicitly present, but the files also recommend workload identity for production. The risk is medium because misuse depends on user privileges and deployment context.

medium_findings.title: AgentID Sidecar Exposure Risk
medium_findings.description: The sidecar documentation includes unauthenticated token endpoints and development port mappings. It also warns to keep the service local, so the main risk is misconfiguration that exposes token acquisition endpoints beyond the pod or Docker network.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The unauthenticated endpoints and port mapping are concrete, and the mitigation is documented nearby. This supports a medium-confidence configuration risk, not malicious behavior.

low_findings.title: Static Command Execution Findings Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: Most external command detections are Markdown code blocks or inline command references for PowerShell, Azure CLI, Python package installation, and Graph examples. No evidence found that the skill itself executes these commands automatically.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited content is visibly instructional Markdown, and no executable helper script was found among the scanned files. The commands remain sensitive only if a user runs them with tenant privileges.

low_findings.title: Scanner False Positives From Identity Terminology
low_findings.description: Several severe static hits are semantic false positives: SAML was treated as Windows SAM, UUID generation was treated as weak cryptography, and relative Markdown links or API paths were treated as filesystem traversal. No evidence found of local SAM access, weak cryptographic use, or path traversal logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding context shows identity protocol terms and safe token validation settings. These static matches do not map to the dangerous behaviors named by the analyzer.

low_findings.title: No Prompt Injection Attempt Found
low_findings.description: A targeted search for override language, fake system instructions, pre-approval claims, and requests to skip security review found no evidence in SKILL.md or reference files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The inspected skill metadata and content did not include suspicious instruction-override text. Confidence is high, though it is based on text search and manual review rather than a formal parser.

dangerous_patterns.title: External CLI Invocation in Provisioning Example
dangerous_patterns.description: A Python example invokes the Azure CLI with subprocess.run to obtain the signed-in user ID. The arguments are fixed, so command injection is not evident, but it depends on the caller Azure CLI context.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess call is real within an example and uses fixed arguments. It is a moderate operational risk because it can act with the current Azure CLI identity if copied and run.

dangerous_patterns.title: PowerShell Credential Creation Example
dangerous_patterns.description: A PowerShell example calls Invoke-MgGraphRequest to add a password credential to a Blueprint. This is intended setup guidance, but it creates a secret that must be protected immediately.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command and secret output are explicit. The same section labels the secret flow as local development, reducing confidence that this is unsafe by intent.

dangerous_patterns.title: Network Token Requests With Client Secret
dangerous_patterns.description: The token exchange example posts client credentials and fmi_path values to the Entra token endpoint. This is required for the documented flow, but it is sensitive because mishandled secrets or logs could expose credentials.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The network request and client_secret parameter are clear. The endpoint is Microsoft Entra and not suspicious, so this is sensitive legitimate behavior.

content.value_statement: Agent identity setup requires precise Graph calls, token flows, and tenant permissions. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through Entra Agent ID provisioning and runtime authentication.
actual_capabilities: Explains when to use Agent Identity Blueprints, BlueprintPrincipals, and per-instance Agent Identities.
Provides Microsoft Graph endpoints for creating Blueprints, BlueprintPrincipals, Agent Identities, and permission grants.
Documents autonomous and on-behalf-of token exchange using fmi_path and /.default scopes.
Shows setup guidance for Workload Identity Federation, client-secret development flows, and the AgentID sidecar.
Lists common Agent ID errors with likely causes and fixes.
Summarizes known product limitations and permission constraints for planning deployments.
limitations: It is documentation guidance and does not validate live tenant configuration by itself.
Graph request bodies and preview API behavior must be checked against current Microsoft Learn documentation.
Examples require privileged Entra roles and should be tested in non-production tenants first.
Client secret examples are intended for local development, not production deployment.

use_cases.title: Provision Agent Identities
use_cases.description: Create the Blueprint, BlueprintPrincipal, and per-agent service principals with the required Graph permissions.

use_cases.title: Implement Runtime Token Exchange
use_cases.description: Build autonomous or OBO token exchange flows that produce auditable Graph tokens for each agent instance.

use_cases.title: Deploy Polyglot Agent Authentication
use_cases.description: Use the AgentID sidecar with local-only networking, workload identity, and downstream API configuration.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Basic Agent ID Setup
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Entra Agent ID skill to outline the minimum steps for creating a Blueprint, BlueprintPrincipal, and one Agent Identity in a test tenant.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a Credential Strategy
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare Workload Identity Federation, Managed Identity, and client secret options for my Entra Agent ID deployment. Recommend one for development and one for production.

prompt_templates.title: Design OBO Permissions
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me design an OBO flow for an agent that needs User.Read and Tasks.ReadWrite through Microsoft Graph. Include required Blueprint settings and grants.

prompt_templates.title: Review a Sidecar Deployment
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my AgentID sidecar deployment plan for token exposure, network binding, credential source, and Graph permission risks.

output_examples.input: I need one identity per support agent instance.
output_examples.output: A step-by-step provisioning plan that separates the shared Blueprint from each Agent Identity and grants permissions per instance.

output_examples.input: My OBO exchange fails with an assertion validation error.
output_examples.output: A troubleshooting checklist that verifies the user token audience, Blueprint API scope, optional claims, and delegated grants.

output_examples.input: I want to run the AgentID sidecar in Kubernetes.
output_examples.output: A deployment review focused on localhost binding, workload identity, secret avoidance, health probes, and downstream Graph configuration.

best_practices: Use Workload Identity Federation or managed identity for production credentials.
Grant Graph permissions to each Agent Identity, not to the BlueprintPrincipal.
Keep the AgentID sidecar reachable only from the local pod or trusted container network.
anti_patterns: Do not expose the sidecar through a public load balancer or ingress.
Do not use Azure CLI tokens for Agent Identity APIs.
Do not place credentials directly on Agent Identity service principals.

faq.question: What is this skill for?
faq.answer: It helps plan and implement Microsoft Entra Agent ID provisioning, permissions, and token exchange flows.

faq.question: Does it create resources automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples that a user may run in an authorized tenant.

faq.question: Which tools can use it?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Is it suitable for production secrets?
faq.answer: Production deployments should use workload identity or managed identity, not copied client-secret examples.

faq.question: Why is the audit risk medium?
faq.answer: The examples are legitimate but can create credentials, grant permissions, and exchange tokens when run with tenant privileges.

faq.question: What should users verify before running examples?
faq.answer: They should verify Microsoft Graph endpoints, tenant roles, requested scopes, and current Microsoft Learn guidance.
